title: Wet-felted coin purse
subtitle: A pocket-shaped purse around 10 by 12 cm, felted over a resist with a sewn zip or button-and-loop closure
excerpt: Wet-felt a small coin purse over a flat resist that creates the pocket shape. The resist is removed partway through fulling, leaving an opening that can be fitted with a hand-sewn zip or a button-and-loop closure. The finished purse is around 10 by 12 cm and firm enough to hold its shape in a bag.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the resist-felting tradition for small 3D forms. Resist-felted purse construction is standard guild practice; the method is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for spinning or felting. The staples lie roughly parallel along the ribbon's length, making it easy to pull thin wisps and lay them evenly.

A flat template placed inside a layered wool layout to prevent the two faces from felting together, creating a pocket shape. The resist defines the internal form; removing it before final fulling opens the pocket for finishing.

The vigorous agitation phase of wet felting that follows initial surface bonding. Rolling, rubbing, and throwing compact the fibres fully, shrink the piece to its finished dimensions, and firm the felt to a dense, stable fabric.

Wool that has bonded lightly enough to hold its shape and be cut or manipulated without fraying, but is still somewhat open in structure. Pre-felt is the correct stage at which to remove the resist before continuing to full.

content.text: A wet-felted coin purse is made by laying merino
roving
on both sides of a flat
resist

cut to the planned internal size of the purse. The resist prevents the front and back faces from bonding together during the initial felting stage, creating the hollow pocket. The resist is removed before final
fulling

, so the pocket walls continue to compact without the resist distorting the shape. The finished purse is approximately 10 cm wide by 12 cm tall when closed, with walls firm enough to hold coins and cards without collapsing.

The resist should be cut to approximately 14 cm wide by 17 cm tall to account for the 25 per cent linear shrinkage. Use dense foam, thick felt, or a double-folded plastic folder. The resist must be stiff enough to hold its shape when wet wool is rubbed over it, but flexible enough to be rolled up and extracted through a small slit. A yoga mat off-cut at 5 to 6 mm thickness is good.
What you need

items.name: Merino roving, contrasting colour (optional, for surface detail)
Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)
Dense foam sheet or stiff plastic (for resist)
Short zip (12 cm) or button and strong thread for closure
Blunt darning needle and waxed thread for sewing

content.text: Method
Step 1: Cut the resist and lay out the first face

Cut the resist to 14 cm wide by 17 cm tall. Round the bottom two corners slightly; a rounded base prevents the corners from pulling thin during fulling. Lay the resist flat on the bamboo mat. Pull thin wisps of merino and lay two cross-hatched layers over the front face of the resist, extending 2 to 3 cm beyond the resist edge on all sides. The overhanging fibres will fold around the edge and bond with the back face, forming the seam. Check the layout for even thickness; hold it up to a light source if needed.
Step 2: Wet down the front face

Mix a light soapy solution and pour it over the front face from the centre outward to saturate without disturbing the roving. Lay felting net over the top if available. Rub through the net with flat hands in circular motions for 4 to 5 minutes, keeping the surface hot. The roving should begin to flatten and the surface should start to feel cohesive. Remove the net.
Step 3: Fold the overhang and lay out the back face

Carefully lift the mat, flip it, and lay it down with the wet face now on the mat surface. Fold the overhanging roving from all four sides back over the resist, pressing each flap down flat. These folded edges become the seam of the purse. Leave the top edge of the purse unfolded on one side; this will form the opening. Now lay two cross-hatched layers of roving over the newly exposed back face of the resist, again extending 2 to 3 cm beyond all sides except the top. Wet down and work as before for another 4 to 5 minutes.
Step 4: Work to pre-felt stage
Keep working both faces, flipping every 3 to 4 minutes. Keep the water hot throughout. After 20 to 25 minutes the surface should be at
pre-felt

stage: the fabric holds together when lifted and does not pull apart under light pinching. Do not over-work at this stage. If the fabric is too dense before the resist is removed, extracting the resist will distort the opening.
Step 5: Remove the resist

Cut a horizontal slit along the top opening of the purse, through the felt only, running the full width. Keep the cut neat and parallel to the top edge. Reach in and gently roll the resist up on itself or fold it, then ease it out through the slit. Take care not to stretch the opening. Once the resist is out, press the opening closed with your fingers and work the cut edge lightly with wet soapy hands to begin closing the surface fibres.
Step 6: Full to final size

Roll the purse inside the bamboo mat and roll back and forth on the worktop with firm hand pressure for 3 to 4 minutes. Unroll, rotate 90 degrees, and roll again. Throw the purse firmly onto the mat surface 10 to 15 times periodically to shock the fibres. Check the dimensions every few minutes and continue until the purse measures approximately 10 cm wide by 12 cm tall and the walls feel dense and firm throughout. This stage typically takes a further 20 to 25 minutes.
Step 7: Rinse and shape

Rinse thoroughly in hot water, then plunge into cold water for 30 seconds. Squeeze out the water by pressing the purse between both palms. Shape by hand: press the side walls flat, square off the corners if needed, and open the top edge into a clean straight line. Stuff the purse lightly with dry cloth while drying to keep the shape. Dry flat or propped open. Once dry, trim any loose surface fibres at the opening with sharp scissors.
Step 8: Fit the closure

For a zip closure: lay a 12 cm zip along the inside of the opening with the zip teeth sitting at the felt edge. Pin or clip in place. Thread a blunt darning needle with strong waxed thread and sew the zip tape to the felt with a running stitch along both edges of the zip tape. Felt takes a needle well without needing a thimble. Pull each stitch firm but not so tight that the felt puckers. Work both sides of the zip tape before testing the zip action.

For a button-and-loop closure: sew a button firmly to the front face of the purse, 1 cm below the opening centre. On the back face at the same position, sew a loop of strong thread or thin cord long enough to pass over the button. Felt holds button stitching well; work through the full thickness of the wall for security.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The front and back faces of the purse felted together despite the resist
items.cause: The roving overhang was folded incorrectly, allowing fibres from both faces to meet in the pocket area before the resist was removed
items.fix: After folding the overhang, check that no stray fibres cross the opening plane to the opposite face. Trim any crossover wisps with scissors before wetting the second face.

items.problem: The opening tore when the resist was removed
items.cause: The felt was worked to full density before the resist was extracted
items.fix: Remove the resist at pre-felt stage, not after full density. The cut slit needs to be generous enough to allow the resist to slide or roll out without catching.

items.problem: The purse walls are thin and floppy rather than firm
items.cause: The total roving weight was insufficient, or fulling was stopped too early
items.fix: 40 g of merino is the minimum for this purse size. If the walls feel thin after fulling, re-wet and continue working; felt does not deteriorate if you return to it later.

items.problem: The purse is wider than it is tall after fulling
items.cause: Rolling direction was not rotated, so the piece shrank more along one axis
items.fix: Rotate the rolling direction 90 degrees every 3 to 4 minutes during fulling. Also check the orientation before rolling: the long axis of the purse should be perpendicular to the rolling direction on each pass.

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a wool wash, pressing gently rather than rubbing. Reshape while damp; stuff lightly to maintain the pocket shape while drying. Avoid machine washing; the agitation will continue to shrink and compress the felt. A zip should be left open during washing to prevent the tape from distorting the felt. The zip stitching can be reinforced with a few extra passes of waxed thread if the thread shows signs of wear. Felt does not fray, so raw edges at the opening will not unravel in use.
